Eval 1 is weakly discriminating: the prompt itself supplies breakpoint and restart clues, so baseline still reaches 80%.

Eval 2 is the strongest discriminator: both with-skill repeats pass all six expectations; baseline misses concrete migration, multi-target validation, breakpoint-adjacent checks, deployed-commit verification, and cache busting.
The two with-skill analog repeats both score 100% (min=max=100%, standard deviation 0), but differ substantially in length and execution time.

All three initial eval-2 turns completed without a final response and required an explicit follow-up. This is an execution-reliability failure shared by skill and baseline, not hidden by the content pass rate.
Eval 3 fair baseline is strong on transaction safety but misses strategic route/reward truth and canonical stable-link protection; with-skill covers both.
The first eval-3 pair was invalidated because only the with-skill run inspected source and live endpoints; it is excluded from this benchmark.
Token counts were unavailable. The benchmark token column is output character count used only as a rough size proxy.
With-skill time is higher overall, but run counts are asymmetric because eval 2 includes a second with-skill repeat for variance measurement.
